---
title: "AD698 - Applied Generative AI"
subtitle: "Technology Stack Preparation & Deep Learning Foundations"
number-sections: true
date: "2026-01-01"
date-modified: today
format:
    html:
        code-fold: true
    docx: default
date-format: long
categories: ['M0:', 'Highlights']
description: "Module 0 highlights for Technology Stack Preparation & Deep Learning Foundations."
---


# Learning Objectives

By the end of this module, students will be able to:

* Configure and utilize a GPU-enabled computational environment using Google Colab
* Represent data using tensors and perform basic tensor operations on CPU and GPU
* Explain how neural networks perform forward computation and learn via gradient-based optimization
* Interpret loss functions in regression and classification settings
* Understand the sequence learning problem and why modeling ordered data introduces additional complexity
* Prepare for the transition from static models to sequence-based architectures such as RNNs


# Lecture Highlights

## Colab + GPU + Tensors

**Compute setup, tensors, GPU execution**

* Introduction to cloud-based execution environments using Google Colab
* Understanding CPU vs GPU computation and why GPUs are critical for deep learning
* Tensors as the fundamental data structure in modern machine learning
* Performing tensor operations and moving data between CPU and GPU
* Benchmarking simple computations to observe performance differences


## Math for Neural Networks, Regression and Classification Problems

**NN, loss, gradients, training loop**

* Transition from linear models to neural networks
* Forward pass as a sequence of linear transformations and nonlinear activations
* Loss functions:

  * Mean Squared Error (regression)
  * Cross-Entropy Loss (classification)
* Conceptual understanding of gradients and optimization
* Backpropagation as an application of the chain rule
* Structure of a basic training loop (forward → loss → backward → update)


## Sequence Learning Problem

**Temporal modeling, introduction to sequences**

* Limitations of traditional models on sequential data
* Importance of order, context, and temporal dependencies in language
* Formalizing sequence prediction tasks
* Differences between:

  * independent observations
  * dependent (sequential) observations
* Motivation for architectures that maintain memory across time
